title: What is a Neural Network?
concept: Formalizing the vocabulary: a neural network is simply multiple perceptrons — now called neurons — connected together in layers, each layer feeding its results into the next.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In the network that solves XOR, how many hidden perceptrons are needed, and what layer do they form?

options.text: Two hidden perceptrons, forming the hidden layer
One perceptron, forming the output layer only
Four perceptrons, one for each XOR input pair

exercises.explain: Two hidden perceptrons form the hidden layer that feeds into the single output perceptron.

cards.title: Time to give it a name
cards.body: You've built input values feeding into a hidden layer, feeding into an output perceptron. That whole connected structure has an official name — and you're about to learn it.

cards.title: What is a neural network?
cards.body: A neural network is a group of simple perceptron-like units — now usually called neurons — connected together in layers, where each layer's outputs feed forward into the next layer as inputs.

cards.title: Like a company's org chart
cards.body: The input layer is like frontline staff receiving raw requests. The hidden layer(s) are like middle management, processing and combining that information. The output layer is the final decision-maker who announces the answer.

cards.title: Naming the layers you already built
cards.body: In your XOR network: the two input numbers form the input layer, the OR-style and NAND-style perceptrons form a hidden layer of two neurons, and the AND-style perceptron is the output layer. Put together, that's a complete, small neural network.

cards.title: It doesn't think like a brain
cards.body: The word "neural" borrows from biological neurons in your brain, but don't overthink the comparison. A neural network here is just weighted sums and thresholds arranged in layers — there's no consciousness, no biology, just simple math repeated many times.

left.text:
One single unit
Makes one small decision

right.text:
Many neurons, layered
Connected decisions add up

options.text: Multiple perceptron-like neurons connected together in layers, each feeding forward into the next
A single perceptron renamed for marketing purposes
A database that stores training examples
A truth table with more rows added

exercises.explain: A neural network is neurons connected in layers, each layer passing its output forward.

exercises.prompt: True or false: In modern terminology, an individual perceptron-like unit inside a neural network is usually called a "neuron."
exercises.explain: True — "neuron" is the standard term for one of these connected units inside a network.

exercises.sentence: A neural network is built from an input layer, one or more hidden layers, and an ___ layer.
exercises.explain: Every neural network has an input layer, hidden layer(s), and a final output layer.

exercises.prompt: Which statement correctly uses neural network vocabulary?

options.text: "The hidden layer's neurons feed their outputs into the output layer."
"The output layer feeds data backward into the input layer before training even starts."
"A neural network can only ever contain exactly one neuron."

exercises.explain: Data flows forward: input layer to hidden layer(s) to output layer — each layer's neurons pass results to the next.

exercises.prompt: A friend says, "A neural network is basically a tiny digital copy of a human brain that thinks the way we do." Using accurate neural network vocabulary, how would you correct them?

options.text: Not quite — a neural network just borrows the word "neuron" from biology; it's really layers of simple perceptron-like units doing weighted sums, with no actual thinking or consciousness involved
They're completely correct, neural networks are identical to brains
Neural networks have nothing at all to do with the word "neuron" and the name is a total coincidence
A neural network only becomes brain-like once it has an output layer

exercises.explain: Exactly — the naming is inspired by biology, but a neural network is layers of neurons (perceptron-like units) doing straightforward math, not an actual simulated brain.
